title: Building a simple timber garden bench
subtitle: Construct a slatted garden bench from treated softwood
excerpt: How to build a basic rectangular garden bench with two legs at each end, two stretchers, and a slatted seat, using pressure-treated sawn softwood, galvanised fixings, and simple halved or bolted joints, without specialist woodworking tools.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. RHS garden furniture guidance. Forest Garden UK construction notes. Timber Research and Development Association outdoor timber treatment notes.

glossaryTerms.definition: A horizontal structural member connecting two legs of a piece of furniture, positioned below the seat. On a bench, two stretchers — one connecting each pair of end legs — prevent the legs from splaying. A lower stretcher between the two end frames adds further rigidity.

A bolt with a domed head and a square neck beneath the head, designed to be drawn through a pre-drilled hole so that the square neck bites into the timber and prevents the bolt rotating while the nut is tightened. Galvanised (zinc-plated) versions are required for outdoor timber construction — uncoated steel corrodes and stains the wood within one season.

The space between adjacent seat slats on outdoor furniture, typically 6 to 10 mm. The gap allows rainwater to drain rather than pooling on the seat surface, which would accelerate timber decay. A 10 mm gap also allows the timber to expand across its width in damp conditions without buckling.

content.text: A simple garden bench: 1500 mm long by 400 mm wide at a seat height of 430 to 450 mm: can be built from two lengths of standard 75 × 75 mm and 100 × 25 mm pressure-treated sawn softwood. The construction uses
at the leg-to-stretcher joints rather than screws, which would eventually work loose under the dynamic load of sitting and standing. The
should be checked with a 10 mm spacer during assembly.

From 75 × 75 mm treated softwood, cut four legs at 450 mm and two stretchers at 380 mm. From 100 × 25 mm treated softwood, cut two seat rails at 1500 mm and five seat slats at 420 mm. Mark all cuts with the combination square before sawing. Stack and label the cut pieces.
Step 2, assemble the two end frames
Pair the legs and position one

between each pair at a height of 120 mm from the bottom of the legs. Clamp the assembly on a flat surface. Drill through each leg and stretcher with a 10 mm bit and drive a 10 × 100 mm galvanised coach bolt through the leg and stretcher at each joint, fitting a washer under the nut. Check each end frame is square before the bolts are fully tightened.
Step 3, join the two end frames with the seat rails

Stand both end frames upright and fix the two long seat rails flush with the top of the legs, one rail to each side face of the legs, using galvanised coach bolts or 100 mm galvanised screws pre-drilled to avoid splitting. The two rails form the long sides of the seat frame. Check the bench is square in plan by measuring both diagonals.

Lay the five seat slats across the seat rails, spacing them evenly with a 10 mm gap between each. Use a piece of 10 mm plywood as a consistent spacer. Fix each slat to both seat rails with two 65 mm galvanised screws at each end, pre-drilling to prevent splitting. Position the screws 15 mm from the end of each slat and countersink the heads flush.

Sand all exposed edges and surfaces with the sanding block to remove splinters and break sharp arris edges. Apply a coat of exterior decking oil or fence treatment to all surfaces, paying particular attention to end grain where moisture enters fastest. Allow to dry fully before use. Treat annually to maintain the timber.

A back can be added by extending the rear legs above seat height and fixing two or three horizontal back rails between them. The back leg extensions should be 450 to 500 mm above the seat rail, giving a comfortable back angle when the rear legs are raked back 15° from vertical. Cutting the rake requires either a compound angle cut or a wedge-shaped spacer block under the rear stretcher.

attrs.symptom: Bench rocks on an uneven surface
attrs.cause: Legs not all the same length; leg ends not cut perfectly square.
attrs.fix: Place the bench on a known-flat surface and identify the short leg. Fit a furniture glide or pad to the short leg foot. For a permanent fix, pack or shim the short leg, scribe and trim the other three legs to match.

attrs.symptom: Seat slats splitting at the fixing screws after one season
attrs.cause: Screws driven without pilot holes; screws too close to the end grain.
attrs.fix: Replace split slats. Always pre-drill pilot holes slightly smaller than the screw shank. Position screws no closer than 15 mm from the end of each slat.

attrs.symptom: Bench becomes wobbly after a season outdoors
attrs.cause: Coach bolt joints loosened by seasonal movement of the timber; screws loosened by repeated dynamic load.
attrs.fix: Re-tighten all coach bolt nuts with a spanner and all screw heads with the drill driver. Treat annually with a penetrating oil to reduce moisture movement in the timber.
